topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the entryway table, stark white paper wrapping a collection of deep purple irises and pale yellow lilies. There was no note. Just the flowers, perfectly arranged, radiating a silent, almost heavy presence in the otherwise mundane hallway. I picked up the box, the paper cool beneath my fingertips, and my breath hitched. I turned it over in my hands, searching for any clue, any indication of who might have sent them, but there was nothing. A prickle started at the base of my neck, a strange tension tightening my shoulders. I set the box down very carefully, as if the petals themselves might be fragile, and retreated to the living room, my gaze flicking toward the front door every few seconds, waiting for a sound, a knock, anything to explain this unsolicited gesture.

[story 2]

When I opened the package, the scent hit me first—intense, almost overpowering, like a garden left too long in the sun. Roses, crimson and velvety, tumbled out, mixed with sprigs of silver foliage. My hand trembled slightly as I examined the arrangement. No inscription, no signature, nothing to ground the gift in reality. I stared at the vibrant blooms, my eyes darting around the apartment, cataloging shadows and corners. A low hum seemed to vibrate in the air, a subtle frequency that made the fine hairs on my arms stand up. I backed away from the table, my heart beginning a quick, uneven rhythm against my ribs, convinced that whatever this delivery represented was far more complicated than simple affection.

[story 3]

It was placed on my desk, directly in front of my monitor. A bouquet of sunflowers, ridiculously bright against the muted grey of my workspace. I didn't touch them immediately. I watched them, feeling a distinct sense of being observed, as if the cheerful yellow petals were somehow watching my every move. I leaned back in my chair, trying to appear casual, but my jaw felt tight. Every time I shifted my weight, I felt a distinct awareness of being scrutinized. I reached out a hesitant finger, hovering just above a large, golden bloom, before snatching my hand back. The silence in the room amplified the sound of my own shallow breathing.
